2.1  Smart Contract Vulnerabilities
Smart contracts are self-executing digital agreements written in c
terms when specific conditions are met [31]. These contracts opera
applications (DApps) and decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols [
contract vulnerabilities can lead to significant financial losses if
In this work, we focus on detecting four critical vulnerability
• Transaction-Ordering Vulnerability (TOV): This vuln
action depends on the order in which transactions are m
execution sequences for profit [9, 36].
• Access Control Vulnerability (ACV): This occurs when
checks, potentially allowing unauthorized users to execut
• Self-Destruct Vulnerability (SDV): This vulnerability
mechanism inappropriately, which can lead to permanen
• Timestamp Dependency Vulnerability (TDV): This vu
tamps for critical operations, which miners can manipula
The prevalence of these vulnerabilities in smart contracts, esp
[8, 24], highlights the urgent need for effective detection techniq
2.2  Control Flow Graphs for Smart Contracts
A CFG is a representation of all paths that might be traversed
context of smart contracts, CFGs provide valuable structural info
identify potential vulnerability patterns [9].
Traditional approaches to vulnerability detection often rely
the control flow structure, which can lead to false positives or
this limitation by recovering the CFG from the contract bytec
contain potential vulnerabilities. This approach enables more targ
analyzing the entire contract code [21, 33].
